Row1.Pathway ID/Name: Reactome: Signaling by MET / MET promotes cell motility / MET activates PTK2 signaling
Row1.Rationale: MET exon 14 skipping (METex14) and MET amplification drive constitutive MET activation and downstream motility/FAK (PTK2) signaling; tepotinib, a highly selective type Ib MET TKI, potently inhibits MET autophosphorylation and MET‑dependent migration/invasion, producing tumor regression in MET‑addicted models and durable clinical responses in METex14 NSCLC.

Row2.Rationale: MET activates RAS–MAPK; tepotinib suppresses ERK phosphorylation and MAPK‑driven proliferation when MET is the dominant driver, but co‑occurring KRAS mutations, KRAS amplification, or MAPK pathway activation provide an alternative proliferative route and are repeatedly associated with primary and acquired resistance to MET TKIs, including tepotinib.

Row3.Rationale: MET signals via PI3K–AKT–mTOR to support survival and growth; tepotinib blocks MET‑dependent PI3K/AKT phosphorylation and induces apoptosis in MET‑altered models, whereas activating alterations in PI3K pathway components or EGFR/PIK3CA‑driven signaling can sustain AKT activity despite MET inhibition and are linked to resistance.

Row4.Rationale: MET activation induces STAT signaling, including STAT3, promoting proliferation and anti‑apoptotic programs; by inhibiting MET kinase activity, tepotinib reduces proximal phosphorylation events and attenuates JAK/STAT output in MET‑addicted tumors, contributing to growth inhibition and tumor regression.

Row5.Pathway ID/Name: MSigDB/Reactome: METex14‑driven cell‑cycle and DNA replication programs
Row5.Rationale: Transcriptomic profiling defines a “MET‑Driven” molecular subtype of METex14 NSCLC enriched for cell‑cycle and DNA‑replication pathways tightly coupled to MET signaling; patients with this subtype derive superior progression‑free survival on MET TKIs, indicating that tepotinib is particularly effective when proliferative programs are MET‑dependent.

Row6.Rationale: A distinct “FGFR‑Activated” subtype of METex14 NSCLC shows FGFR1–4 overexpression and enrichment of FGFR and PI3K‑cascade gene sets; FGF–FGFR signaling can drive tumor progression independently of MET, reducing MET addiction and limiting the efficacy of tepotinib monotherapy, with data suggesting a need for combined MET–FGFR blockade in this context.

Row7.Pathway ID/Name: Bypass RTK signaling (EGFR, ERBB2/HER2, ERBB3/HER3, NTRK, others)
Row7.Rationale: “Bypass‑Activated” METex14 tumors are enriched for oncogenic RTK pathways such as NTRK, NOTCH, ERBB2, and PI3K–AKT/MAPK; clinical and preclinical evidence indicates that baseline copy‑number gains or upregulation of alternate RTKs sustain downstream signaling under MET inhibition and underlie incomplete responses and acquired resistance to tepotinib.

Row8.Rationale: SHP2 integrates signals from multiple RTKs, including MET, to MAPK, PI3K/AKT, and JAK/STAT; in tepotinib‑resistant MET‑altered cell lines, resistance emerges without new MET mutations but with increased RTK signaling, and pharmacologic SHP2 inhibition synergizes with tepotinib in vitro and in xenografts, delaying or reversing resistance by blocking convergent downstream signaling.

Row9.Rationale: In KRAS‑mutant NSCLC, ENO1‑dependent activation of MET and LRP5/6 promotes Wnt/β‑catenin signaling and sustains ERK/AKT activity; tepotinib combined with omeprazole reduces LRP5/6 phosphorylation, Wnt activity, and downstream ERK/AKT, achieving synergistic growth inhibition and tumor regression, indicating Wnt–LRP5/6 as a bypass route that can be co‑targeted.

Row10.Rationale: METex14 and MET‑amplified NSCLC frequently exhibit high PD‑L1 expression, IFN‑γ–related signatures, and immune‑activated microenvironments; these features correlate with benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors, while their impact on intrinsic tepotinib sensitivity is indirect, mainly informing sequencing or combination of MET TKIs with ICIs.

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: DNA damage response (DDR) pathways (MSigDB DDR gene sets)
Row11.Rationale: MET‑amplified NSCLC shows elevated tumor mutation burden, increased neoantigen load, and higher prevalence of DDR pathway alterations, which associate with immune activation and improved outcomes on ICIs; available data do not show direct modulation of DDR by tepotinib, but support integrating MET inhibition with immunotherapy in selected DDR‑altered, MET‑amplified tumors.

Row12.Pathway ID/Name: MET‑driven brain metastasis signaling / blood–brain barrier penetration
Row12.Rationale: Tepotinib crosses the blood–brain barrier and demonstrates strong antitumor activity in MET‑dependent brain metastasis models, with clinical reports of intracranial responses in METex14 NSCLC; suppression of MET signaling within CNS lesions underlies sensitivity in this sanctuary site and supports use of tepotinib for MET‑driven brain metastases.
